mnemonics: FOIL (First-Order Inductive Learner): Imagine a knight FOILing an evil dragon by learning the first rule of combat.
Horn Clause Herald: Picture a herald announcing a new royal rule that always starts with a positive conclusion (head) and lists the conditions (body) like a proclamation.
Coverage Quest: Think of a greedy king who wants his rule to cover as much of his kingdom (positive examples) as possible while banishing the traitors (negative examples).
keyPoints: First-order rules use logical expressions with variables (e.g., Parent(X,Y)) to represent relationships, not just fixed attributes.
A common rule structure is a Horn clause: Head :- Body, meaning the Head is true if all the conditions in the Body are true.

The general-to-specific search (e.g., by FOIL algorithm) starts with an empty body and greedily adds the most constraining literal to maximize positive example coverage and minimize negative example coverage.
commonMistakes: Confusing variable scope: Forgetting that a variable introduced in a literal is universally quantified for the entire rule, not just that one part.
Overfitting on noisy data: Creating an overly long, specific rule that perfectly fits the training data but fails to generalize to new examples.
Ignoring negation: Forgetting that learning from negative examples (what the rule should NOT cover) is just as critical as learning from positive ones.
